_purpose: Seasonal calendar profile for the WI Southern Driftless regional cluster. Month-by-month agricultural, social, economic, and weather patterns across three eras (1830-1960). The cluster's economic calendar shifted three times: lead mining (year-round), wheat monoculture (spring-fall), and dairy farming (365 days, twice daily). The Driftless terrain added its own seasonal complications — coulee flooding in spring, ridgetop wind exposure in winter, and the relentless haying on steep hillside pastures in summer.
display_name: WI Southern Driftless — Seasonal Calendar Profile
primary_economy: Lead mining (1830-1850), wheat monoculture (1840-1875), dairy farming (1875-1960)

calendar_eras.description: The wheat-era calendar dominated the cluster from the 1840s through the 1870s, with the dairy transition beginning in Green County as early as the 1870s and spreading across the cluster by the 1890s. Wheat was the universal cash crop — planted in spring, harvested in late summer, threshed in fall, and sold at the elevator or shipped by railroad. The year's income depended on the weather between April and September. The growing season in the Southern Driftless was 140-160 frost-free days — longer than Minnesota but still tight. Chinch bugs, rust, and soil exhaustion destroyed the wheat economy by the 1870s, forcing the transition to dairy that the Swiss at New Glarus had already demonstrated.

january.agricultural: No field work — ground frozen 2-3 feet deep, snow cover typical. Livestock feeding: cattle, horses, hogs, and chickens consumed stored hay, oats, and corn. Wood cutting for fuel consumed much of the winter labor. In the mining counties (Iowa, Lafayette, Grant), some miners continued underground work through the winter, as the temperature underground was stable. Equipment repair in the barn.
january.social_civic: Deep winter but less isolated than the Minnesota prairies — the Driftless hills sheltered some roads and the Mississippi River towns maintained some commerce. Church attendance when roads passable. Norwegian husandakt (home devotions) in Norwegian settlements. Swiss community gatherings (Vereinsabend) in New Glarus. Cornish miners' chapel meetings continued year-round.
january.economic: No agricultural cash flow. Miners received intermittent pay. Farm families lived on stored food and store credit. Timber harvesting along the Wisconsin River in Sauk County provided some winter employment.
january.weather_conditions: Cold — average 15-25F. Less extreme than Minnesota; the Driftless hills provided some wind shelter compared to the open prairie. Snow cover 6-12 inches typical. The ridgetop farms were exposed to wind; the coulee bottoms were sheltered but prone to temperature inversions that trapped cold air. The Mississippi River usually froze by late December or January.
january.narrative_hook: January in {county} County was wood-cutting season — {ancestor_name} spent as many hours at the woodpile as in the barn, because a Driftless farm burned 12-15 cords of wood a winter, and the ridgetop wind found every crack in the house.

february.agricultural: Same as January — livestock feeding, wood cutting, equipment repair. Maple syrup tapping began in late February in the hardwood-covered coulees — a significant seasonal activity in the Driftless, where sugar maple was abundant on the north-facing slopes. Seed wheat sorted from stored grain.
february.social_civic: Late winter social events: church aid society meetings, box socials at the schoolhouse. The maple sugar camp was a social occasion — families worked together over wood fires boiling sap.
february.economic: Credit accounts at the store continued. Maple sugar provided a small cash or barter income. Some ice harvesting from the Wisconsin River and Driftless lakes.
february.weather_conditions: Continued cold — average 18-28F. February could bring heavy snowfall. The coulee bottoms accumulated drifted snow that blocked roads for days. Late February brought the first hints of thaw on south-facing slopes.
february.narrative_hook: February in the Driftless was maple sugar time — {ancestor_name}'s family tapped the sugar maples on the north slope of the coulee and boiled sap over an open fire, producing the sweetener that replaced molasses in a frontier kitchen.

march.agricultural: Spring thaw began. Coulee-bottom roads became impassable as snowmelt flooded the valleys. Ridgetop roads thawed faster but were rutted. Livestock moved from winter pens. Spring calving began. Plows sharpened, harnesses repaired. In the mining counties, spring flooding sometimes flooded the mine shafts.
march.social_civic: Township annual meeting (March). Church attendance resumed as roads dried. School spring term. Swiss Gemeinderat (community meeting) at New Glarus. Norwegian communities prepared for spring confirmation.
march.economic: Peak credit season — seed, provisions, and supplies purchased on credit against the coming harvest. The merchant's ledger was the bank of the frontier.
march.weather_conditions: Warming rapidly but erratically — 30-50F. Coulee flooding: snowmelt from the ridgetops poured into the narrow valleys, flooding bottom-land farms and roads. The Kickapoo River in Richland County was especially flood-prone. Freeze-thaw cycles made roads treacherous.
march.narrative_hook: March in the Driftless was flood season — the snow melted on the ridgetops and poured into the coulees, and {ancestor_name} watched the creek rise and hoped the bottom-land hay meadow would not be under water for the third year in a row.

april.agricultural: Spring plowing and wheat sowing — the year's most urgent month. As soon as the ground thawed (typically mid-April), every farmer was in the field from dawn to dark. Wheat sown broadcast or with seed drill, then harrowed. Oats sown after wheat. Potatoes planted. Kitchen gardens started. The Driftless ridgetop fields were narrow and required constant turning — plowing was slower per acre than on the flat prairie.
april.social_civic: Easter (variable, March-April). Social activity minimal during planting rush. Confirmation Sunday in Norwegian and Swiss Reformed churches was a major family event.
april.economic: Seed and equipment purchases. Spring labor scarce and expensive. Cheese factory season beginning in the Swiss settlements.
april.weather_conditions: Variable — 40-55F. Late frost possible through mid-April. Spring rains welcome for planted crops. The Driftless microclimates were pronounced: south-facing slopes warmed weeks before north-facing ones, and coulee bottoms were frost pockets that delayed planting.
april.narrative_hook: April in {county} County was the race against the calendar — {ancestor_name} plowed the narrow ridgetop field, turning at the coulee edge where the ground dropped away, knowing that every day lost to wet ground was a bushel lost in September.

may.agricultural: Wheat and oats up and growing. Corn planting (after soil warmed). First cultivation to control weeds. Livestock on pasture — Driftless hillside pastures produced good grass but were steep and rocky. Dairy cows freshened; milk production rising. Kitchen gardens planted after last frost (typically mid-May). Cheese factories opened for the season in the Swiss settlements.
may.social_civic: Norwegian Constitution Day (May 17) celebrated in the Norwegian communities with speeches and community dinners. School year ending. Outdoor social events resumed.
may.economic: Cheese factory checks began — the first cash income for dairy farmers. Swiss cheesemakers opened the factory season when milk supply was sufficient. Egg and butter sales at the store supplemented household income.
may.weather_conditions: Warming steadily — 55-65F. Last killing frost mid-May. Thunderstorms beginning. The Driftless terrain channeled storms through the coulees, creating localized flooding and hail damage.
may.narrative_hook: May in {county} County was when the cheese factory reopened — {ancestor_name} delivered the first full cans of spring milk, and the cheesemaker weighed, tested, and accepted what would become the wheels of Swiss cheese that paid the farm's bills.

june.agricultural: First hay cutting — timothy, clover, and Driftless hillside grass. Hay making in the Driftless was harder than on the prairie: steep hillsides required hand-mowing with a scythe or careful maneuvering with horse-drawn mowers on grades that could overturn equipment. Wheat and oats growing vigorously. Corn cultivation. Dairy at peak production. Garden vegetables available. Cheese factories at full production.
june.social_civic: Summer social season began. Church picnics and ice cream socials. Norwegian midsummer celebrations. Swiss Kinderfest (children's festival) at New Glarus. Rural baseball season.
june.economic: Peak dairy income month — long days and lush pasture produced maximum milk. Cheese factory checks at their highest. Egg production strong.
june.weather_conditions: Warm — 65-80F. Thunderstorms frequent and sometimes violent in the Driftless coulees. Hail a constant worry for the grain crop. Long days (15-16 hours of daylight).
june.narrative_hook: June haying on a Driftless hillside was dangerous work — {ancestor_name} mowed slopes steep enough that a stumble meant a broken leg and a misstep with the team meant an overturned wagon in the coulee bottom.

july.agricultural: Hay making continued — second cutting on good fields. Wheat and oats approaching maturity. Corn in full growth. Garden at peak. Dairy milking continued twice daily. Cheese factories producing wheels of Swiss, Limburger, and brick cheese in Green County. Canning season began — farm women preserved fruits and vegetables for winter.
july.social_civic: Fourth of July — the year's biggest secular celebration. County fair preparation. Church summer programs. In Sauk County, the Ringling Brothers circus (based in Baraboo until 1918) attracted crowds from across the cluster.
july.economic: Pre-harvest. Cheese factory checks steady. Seasonal labor recruited for the coming grain harvest.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 70-85F. Drought risk. Thunderstorms. The Driftless terrain created microclimates: ridgetops were windswept and dry; coulee bottoms were humid and sheltered.
july.narrative_hook: July in the Driftless was when the cheese wheels were piling up in the factory cellar and the grain was turning gold on the ridgetop — {ancestor_name} watched both and calculated: the cheese check was steady, but the grain harvest would make or break the year.

august.agricultural: Grain harvest — wheat, oats, and barley cut and threshed. Threshing crews moved from farm to farm. The Driftless terrain made threshing crew logistics difficult — steam engines had to climb ridges and descend into coulees to reach farms. Corn still growing. Third hay cutting in good years. Dairy milking continued. Canning at full pace in the kitchen.
august.social_civic: County fair — the year's biggest community gathering. Threshing as social event — crews of neighbors working together, enormous meals served by farm women. Church harvest festivals.
august.economic: Grain income. Threshing crew wages: $2.00-$3.00/day plus board. Cheese factory checks continued. Store accounts partially settled with harvest income.
august.weather_conditions: Warm — 68-82F. Rain during harvest was damaging. Ideal harvest weather was warm and dry. Early frost not a concern in August at this latitude.
august.narrative_hook: Threshing day in the Driftless meant the steam engine had to climb the ridge road to reach {ancestor_name}'s farm — and the threshing crew ate like they had been climbing hills all day, because they had.

september.agricultural: Corn harvest began — cutting and shocking, or ear picking. Fall plowing on stubble fields. Potato digging. Root cellar stocked. Apple harvest — orchards on Driftless hillsides produced well. Dairy milking continued. Cheese factories winding down for the season as milk production declined with shorter days and drying pasture.
september.social_civic: School term began. Church fall programs. Norwegian harvest festivals. Social life resumed after harvest pressure.
september.economic: Peak cash income period. Grain sold, threshing settled, cheese factory checks tallied, store credit accounts assessed.
september.weather_conditions: Cooling — 55-68F. First frost late September on exposed ridgetops. The Driftless terrain created frost pockets in coulee bottoms where cold air settled, killing gardens before ridgetop gardens froze.
september.narrative_hook: September in {county} County was apple season on the Driftless hillsides — {ancestor_name} picked Wealthy, Duchess, and Snow apples from trees planted when the farm was new, and the root cellar filled with the winter's fruit.

october.agricultural: Fall plowing until freeze-up. Corn husking. Hog butchering after first hard freeze. Firewood cutting and hauling. Root crops harvested. Dairy milking continued — cows bred for spring calving. Cheese factories closing for the season in the Swiss settlements.
october.social_civic: Peak social month — harvest done, winter not yet arrived. Swiss community dinners. Norwegian lutefisk dinners began. Cornish miners' chapel harvest suppers. Church bazaars. Halloween.
october.economic: Final grain sales. Winter supply purchases — flour, sugar, salt, kerosene. Farm machinery stored and maintained.
october.weather_conditions: Cold — 40-55F. Hard frost by early October. The Driftless hardwoods blazed with fall color — the oaks, maples, and hickories on the coulee slopes created a spectacle that Swiss and Norwegian settlers compared to their homelands' autumn displays. Snow possible by late October.
october.narrative_hook: October in the Driftless was hog-killing weather — {ancestor_name} waited for the first hard freeze, then butchered, salted, and smoked enough pork to carry the family through to spring, while the coulee maples burned red and gold overhead.

november.agricultural: Fall plowing ended when ground froze (typically mid-November). Last corn husking. Firewood hauled. Livestock in winter quarters. Dairy milking continued — the twice-daily chore that never stopped.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving. Church harvest festivals. Deer hunting season — venison supplemented the winter diet. Butchering bees among neighbors.
november.economic: Economic year closing. Store credit accounts assessed. Last purchases before winter.
november.weather_conditions: Cold — 28-40F. Snow becoming persistent. Coulee roads began to drift shut. The ridgetop roads, scoured by wind, were sometimes more passable than the sheltered coulee roads that accumulated deep drifts.
november.narrative_hook: November was when {ancestor_name} measured the year — the grain was sold, the root cellar was full, the hogs were salted, and the cheese factory checks were counted. The math either worked or it did not.

december.agricultural: Dairy milking. Livestock feeding. Wood cutting. Deep winter operations began.
december.social_civic: Christmas — the year's most important social and religious event. Swiss Weihnachten included family gatherings and Reformed Church services. Norwegian julaften (Christmas Eve) with lutefisk, lefse, and hymns in Norwegian. Cornish Christmas with chapel services and pasties. Yankee communities held Congregational and Methodist Christmas services. School Christmas programs. New Year's social gatherings.
december.economic: Minimal activity. Some grain hauling on frozen roads. Store purchases for Christmas.
december.weather_conditions: Cold — 15-28F. Snow cover continuous. The shortest days (9 hours of daylight at the solstice). The Driftless hills sheltered the coulee farms from the worst wind but channeled cold air downslope, creating frost pockets in the valley bottoms.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas in the Driftless brought three traditions to the same hills — Swiss Weihnachten in New Glarus, Norwegian julaften in the western coulees, and Yankee Christmas in the prairie towns — and {ancestor_name}'s family celebrated in the language and tradition of wherever the family came from.

calendar_eras.description: The dairy era calendar was defined by the cow's schedule, not the crop's. Milking twice daily, 365 days a year, at 5 AM and 5 PM, was the inescapable rhythm. The dairy cow never took a holiday. But the dairy economy provided what wheat never had: monthly income. The cheese factory or creamery check arrived every month, smoothing the cash flow that wheat-era farmers had lacked. The cooperative cheese factory — a small building serving 10-20 farms within a few miles — was the economic and social center of rural life. The UW dairy science revolution (Babcock butterfat test, 1890; herd improvement programs; soil conservation) transformed farming from tradition to science. The automobile (after 1910) and rural electrification (after 1935) gradually eased the physical burden, but the twice-daily milking remained until bulk tanks and milking machines became universal in the 1950s.

january.agricultural: Dairy milking twice daily — 5 AM and 5 PM, without exception. Milk hauled to the cheese factory or cream separated on-farm and cream hauled weekly. Livestock feeding. Wood cutting for fuel (or coal stove by the 1920s-1930s). Equipment repair. Ice harvesting from Driftless ponds and the Wisconsin River for summer cooling.
january.social_civic: Winter social life improved by telephone (party lines after 1905) and automobile (after 1910). Church services, aid society meetings, card parties. Annual church business meeting. School in session — children walked the ridgetop roads or rode horse-drawn sleighs.
january.economic: Monthly cheese factory or creamery check — steady income that smoothed the wheat era's feast-or-famine cycle. Store credit supplemented. Ice harvesting provided modest additional income.
january.weather_conditions: Cold — 15-25F average. Driftless terrain provided some shelter from the worst wind. Snow cover 6-12 inches. Coulee roads drifted shut; ridgetop roads passable but windswept. Winter isolation less extreme than Minnesota but still significant for the most remote Richland County ridgetop farms.
january.narrative_hook: January {year}: the alarm clock at 4:30 AM, the lantern in the barn, the warm flank of the cow, and the hiss of milk in the pail — {ancestor_name}'s day started the same way every day of the year in {county} County, because the dairy cow did not recognize holidays, weekends, or blizzards.

february.agricultural: Same as January — dairy chores, livestock feeding, wood cutting. Maple syrup tapping in late February on north-facing coulee slopes. Seed catalogs arrived by mail (RFD). Farm planning for the coming season — UW extension bulletins provided dairy management advice.
february.social_civic: Valentine's box socials. Church sewing circles. Telephone party lines carried news. 4-H clubs organized for the coming year.
february.economic: Monthly dairy check. Seed and supply orders placed. Farm machinery purchases planned — the tractor era was beginning by the 1920s-1930s.
february.weather_conditions: Continued cold. February blizzards possible. Maple sap began to flow on warm days. Days lengthening noticeably.
february.narrative_hook: February in the Driftless: the seed catalog on the kitchen table, the maple sap beginning to drip on the south slope, and the UW extension bulletin in the mailbox explaining why {ancestor_name} should test the butterfat content of every cow in the barn.

march.agricultural: Spring thaw. Coulee flooding — the Kickapoo River and its tributaries flooded annually, sometimes catastrophically. Dairy milking continued. Spring calving. Equipment prepared. Oat seed sorted.
march.social_civic: Township annual meeting. Church spring activities. School spring term.
march.economic: Peak credit season for spring inputs. Dairy check continued.
march.weather_conditions: Warming — 32-48F. Coulee flooding the dominant weather event. Freeze-thaw cycles destroyed roads.
march.narrative_hook: March in the Driftless was the month the Kickapoo rose — {ancestor_name} watched the creek climb the bank and calculated whether the bottom-land hay meadow would be under water for days or weeks.

april.agricultural: Spring plowing and seeding — oats, barley, corn ground prepared. Dairy milking continued. Spring calving in full swing. Pasture greening. Manure spreading. UW county extension agent visited farms to advise on herd improvement and soil management.
april.social_civic: Easter. Confirmation Sunday. Social activity minimal during planting rush.
april.economic: Seasonal labor hired. Dairy check continued. Cheese factory season opening.
april.weather_conditions: Warming — 42-58F. Last frost mid-to-late April. Spring rains.
april.narrative_hook: April: {ancestor_name} was plowing, milking, calving, and spreading manure — all at once, dawn to dark, on a Driftless ridgetop where the furrows ended at the coulee edge.

may.agricultural: Corn planting. Oats growing. Alfalfa first cutting. Dairy cows on full pasture — Driftless hillside pastures provided excellent grass but required rotational management to prevent erosion. Cheese factory at full production. Kitchen garden planted.
may.social_civic: May 17 (Norwegian Constitution Day) in Norwegian communities. Memorial Day. School ending. Swiss Kinderfest.
may.economic: Dairy income rising with pasture quality. Egg production at peak.
may.narrative_hook: May was when the pasture turned the milk rich — {ancestor_name}'s cows on the Driftless hillside grass produced the butterfat that made the Swiss cheese in the valley factory, and the cheese check rose with the grass.

june.agricultural: First hay cutting — the most labor-intensive month. Driftless hillside haying was physically demanding and sometimes dangerous. Horse-drawn mowers on steep slopes required skill and caution. Dairy at peak production. Corn cultivation. Cheese factory producing at maximum.
june.social_civic: Church picnics. Ice cream socials. Swiss Volksfest. Norwegian midsummer. Rural baseball. County extension agent summer tours.
june.economic: Peak dairy income. Cheese factory checks highest. Long days enabled maximum production.
june.narrative_hook: June haying in the Driftless was the hardest work of the year — {ancestor_name} cut the hillside meadow with a mower on a grade steep enough to spill a wagon, raked the hay into windrows that followed the contour, and hauled it to the barn before the thunderstorm that was building over the ridge.

july.agricultural: Second hay cutting. Corn in full growth. Wheat and oats approaching maturity. Dairy milking continued. Garden at peak — canning season began. Cheese factory producing Swiss, brick, and Limburger in Green County.
july.social_civic: Fourth of July. County fair preparation. Wisconsin Dells tourism season (Sauk County). Taliesin tours at Spring Green (Iowa County) after Frank Lloyd Wright established his studio.
july.economic: Pre-harvest. Dairy checks steady. Tourism income in Sauk County growing after 1900.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 72-86F. Drought risk. Thunderstorms through the coulees.
july.narrative_hook: July in {county} County: the corn was shoulder-high, the cheese was aging in the factory cellar, and {ancestor_name} watched the sky for the thunderstorm that could flatten the oat field in ten minutes of hail.

august.agricultural: Small grain harvest and threshing. Threshing crews with steam-powered (later tractor-powered) separators moved from farm to farm. Corn still growing. Dairy milking continued. Canning at full pace.
august.social_civic: County fair. Threshing as community event. Church harvest festivals. Circus season in Baraboo (pre-1918).
august.economic: Grain income from threshing. Dairy checks continued. Threshing crew wages paid.
august.weather_conditions: Warm — 68-82F. Ideal harvest weather: warm and dry.
august.narrative_hook: Threshing day: the separator arrived at {ancestor_name}'s ridgetop farm in {county} County, and the kitchen crew produced dinner for twenty — roast beef, mashed potatoes, pie, and coffee — because feeding the threshing crew was as much a competition among the women as the harvest itself.

september.agricultural: Corn harvest. Fall plowing. Potato digging. Apple picking on Driftless hillside orchards. Silo filling (corn silage for winter feed — silos became standard 1900-1920). Dairy milking continued. Cheese factory production declining as pasture dried.
september.social_civic: School term began. Church fall programs. Harvest festivals.
september.economic: Grain sales. Dairy checks. Store accounts settled. The annual accounting.
september.weather_conditions: Cooling — 55-68F. First frost late September on ridgetops and in coulee frost pockets.
september.narrative_hook: September was the settling month — {ancestor_name} sold the grain, counted the cheese checks, and did the arithmetic. The dairy cows had turned the Driftless grass into a monthly paycheck that the wheat had never provided.

october.agricultural: Fall plowing until freeze-up. Corn picking. Hog butchering after hard freeze. Firewood hauled. Silo filling completed. Dairy cows bred for spring calving.
october.social_civic: Peak social month. Swiss community dinners. Norwegian lutefisk dinners. Church bazaars. Halloween. The Driftless fall color was spectacular — tourists began visiting from Madison by the 1920s.
october.economic: Final grain sales. Winter supplies purchased.
october.weather_conditions: Cold — 42-55F. Hard frost by early October. The coulee hardwoods in full autumn color — oaks, maples, hickories, and walnuts creating a palette that rivaled New England.
october.narrative_hook: October in the Driftless was when the hills earned their beauty — the coulee maples blazed red and gold while {ancestor_name} butchered hogs, filled the root cellar, and counted the cords of wood that stood between the family and the winter.

november.agricultural: Winter operations beginning. Dairy milking continued. Livestock feeding. Last firewood hauled.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving. Deer hunting season. Church Thanksgiving services.
november.economic: Economic year closing. Dairy check continued through winter — the great advantage of the dairy economy.
november.weather_conditions: Cold — 28-40F. Snow becoming persistent. Coulee roads beginning to drift.
november.narrative_hook: November: the cheese factory had closed for the season, but {ancestor_name}'s creamery check still arrived — because the cows still milked, the cream still shipped, and the dairy economy, unlike wheat, did not shut down for winter.

december.agricultural: Dairy milking. Livestock feeding. Wood cutting. Deep winter.
december.social_civic: Christmas — Swiss Weihnachten, Norwegian julaften, Cornish chapel Christmas, Yankee Christmas. Each community celebrated in its own tradition, often in its own language. School Christmas programs. New Year's gatherings.
december.economic: Minimal activity beyond dairy. Creamery check.
december.weather_conditions: Cold — 18-30F. Short days. Snow cover continuous. The Driftless coulee bottoms trapped cold air, creating temperature inversions where the valley floor was colder than the ridgetop.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas in the Driftless was three Christmases — Swiss Weihnachten at the Reformed church in New Glarus, Norwegian julaften in the western coulee chapels, and American Christmas in the Yankee towns — and {ancestor_name}'s family celebrated whichever one their ancestors had brought across the ocean.

narrative_hooks: The dairy cow's schedule was the Driftless farmer's schedule — {ancestor_name} milked at 5 AM and 5 PM every day of the year, and the monthly cheese factory check was the reward for never taking a day off.

Haying in the Driftless was harder than haying on the flat — {ancestor_name} mowed hillsides steep enough to overturn a wagon, raked hay that rolled downhill, and hauled loads up grades that tested the team.

The cheese factory closed for winter, but the creamery check did not — {ancestor_name}'s twice-daily milking earned a monthly check through December and January, which was something the wheat-era farmers had never had.
The Kickapoo River flooded every March, and {ancestor_name} knew the bottom-land hay meadow would be under water — the question was for how many weeks.
Maple sugar season in the Driftless coulees was the first sign of spring — {ancestor_name} tapped the sugar maples on the north slope while the south slope was already showing bare ground.
Fall in the Driftless was when the hills earned their reputation — the oaks and maples blazed red and gold in the coulees while {ancestor_name} butchered hogs and counted the year's arithmetic.
source_references: Apps, Jerry. Wisconsin Agriculture: A History. Madison: Wisconsin Historical Society Press, 2015.
Apps, Jerry. Cheese: The Making of a Wisconsin Tradition. Amherst, WI: Amherst Press, 1998.
Nesbit, Robert C. Wisconsin: A History. 2nd ed. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1989.
Lampard, Eric E. The Rise of the Dairy Industry in Wisconsin. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1963.
Schafer, Joseph. The Wisconsin Lead Region. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1932.
